text: All plants absorb Na+ in the presence of salt. This sodium ion is then spread within the symplast at the cellular and tissue levels and between the organs. Sodium can decrease the water potential of the cell and it may also be harmful to the plant. A number of regulatory mechanisms have been described such as regulation by Ca2+, cNPs, ROS and hormones and regulation of gene and protein expression by transcription and post-transcription modification. This review provides a summary of the movement of Na+ to the intracellular and extracellular space of the plant cells under salt stress and the regulation of the balances of the Na+ fluxes, particularly in the initial phases of salt stress. It has been deduced that the divergence between salt resistant and salt sensitive plants does not arise from a difference in type of transporters but is rather a result of a control on sodium uptake and its balance in the plant tissue.
explanation: The text is a broad, generalized summary with few specific details or citations, a pattern common in AI-generated syntheses. While plausible as human writing, its generic phrasing raises the likelihood of AI authorship.
